The mainstream multi-stream SLR framework extends the pretrained visual module with multi-cue visual information.

The mainstream multi-stream SLR framework extends the pretrained visual module with multi-cue visual information, including global features and regional features such as hands and faces in independent streams.

The theoretical support for this approach comes from sign language linguistics, where sign language utilizes multiple complementary channels (e.g., hand shapes, facial expressions) to convey information.
The multi-cue mechanism essentially exploits hard attention to key information, yielding the current SOTA performances.

The CVT-SLR framework is proposed as a novel contrastive visual-textual transformation framework for SLR to fully explore pretrained knowledge of visual and language modalities.
CVT-SLR is introduced alongside SLR, with CVT-SLR being a framework that aims to fully explore pretrained knowledge of both visual and language modalities.
CVT-SLR keeps the pretrained visual module and introduces a variational autoencoder (VAE) to replace the traditional contextual module.
CVT-SLR replaces the traditional contextual module with a varitional autoencoder (VAE) responsible for learning pretrained contextual knowledge based on a pseudo-translation task.
VAE is an autoencoder that maintains consistency of input and output modalities, playing an implicit cross-modal alignment role.
The contrastive alignment algorithm introduced in the framework focuses on both positive and negative samples to enhance explicit cross-modal consistency constraints.
Extensive quantitative experiments were conducted on public datasets PHOENIX-2014 and PHOENIX-2014T to demonstrate the advance of the proposed CVT-SLR framework.
The proposed CVT-SLR framework introduces fully pretrained language knowledge and a new consistency constraint mechanism, with rigorous testing through ablation study and qualitative analysis.
The proposed framework introduces fully pre-trained language knowledge for the first time.
